records.content: Here we intriduce some useful results of simplified but heuristic field integrations.

1. Standard Gaussian Identity
We start with the auxiliary Gaussian integral for a variable y:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}y^2\right) dy = \sqrt{2\pi}

$$

2. Shift Transformation
Introduce a shift y \to y - cx^2 to couple y with x^2. The integration measure remains unchanged (dy):
$$

\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{2\pi} &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}(y - cx^2)^2\right] dy \
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}y^2 + cyx^2 - \frac{1}{2}c^2 x^4\right) dy \
&= \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}c^2 x^4\right) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}y^2 + cyx^2\right) dy
\end{aligned}

$$

Rearranging to isolate the quartic term:
$$\exp\left(\frac{1}{2}c^2 x^4\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}y^2 + cyx^2\right) dy

$$

3. Coefficient Matching
To match the target term \exp(bx^4), we set \frac{1}{2}c^2 = b, which implies c = \sqrt{2b}. Substituting this back:
$$\exp(bx^4) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}y^2 + \sqrt{2b}\,x^2 y\right) dy

$$

4. Full Integral Transformation
Substitute the above identity into the original integral I = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp(-ax^2 + bx^4) dx:
$$

\begin{aligned}
I &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-ax^2} \left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\frac{1}{2}y^2 + \sqrt{2b},x^2 y} dy \right] dx \
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} dy , e^{-\frac{1}{2}y^2} \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} dx , \exp\left[ -\left(a - y\sqrt{2b}\right)x^2 \right]}_{\text{Gaussian integral over } x}
\end{aligned}

$$

5. Integrating out x
Performing the inner Gaussian integral over x:
$$I = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}y^2\right) \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{a - y\sqrt{2b}}} \, dy

$$

This document proves that based on free-particle Hamiltonian, if we add an attractive potential, the symmetry of
the ground state will be broken. The new ground state will be a bound state.

We have the following Hamiltonian:

$$H = \sum_{i=1}^N \varepsilon_\mathbf{k} c

$$

where p_i is the momentum of the i-th particle, and m is the mass of the particle.

The ground state is of course not free electrons. It is an interesting coherent state where particle number is not definite.

It is constructed as follows:

$$| \psi_{BCS \rangle} = e^{\sum_{k} \phi_{k} c_{k\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k\downarrow}^\dagger} | 0 \rangle

$$

We can not create two electrons with the same spin and momentum so the exponential term is truncated to its 1st order.

$$| \psi_{BCS \rangle} = \prod_{k} \left(1 + \phi_{k} c_{k\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k\downarrow}^\dagger\right) | 0 \rangle

$$

Given different Cooper pair wave functions | \phi_k \rangle, we can construct different kinds of ground states. We notice that this ground state is not normalized.

$$\braket{\psi_{BCS}}{\psi_{BCS}} = \prod_{k} \langle 0 | 1 + \phi^*_{k \rangle c_{-k\downarrow} c_{k\uparrow}} \braket{1 + \phi_{k} c_{k\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k\downarrow}^\dagger}{0}

$$

NOTICE: It’s free to move (1 + \phi_{k} c_{k\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k\downarrow}^\dagger) as long as we keep the order of items with the same momentum.

It’s convenient to define creation and annihilation of Cooper pairs as follows:

$$b_{k}^\dagger \equiv c_{k\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k\downarrow}^\dagger

$$

Of course, vice versa:
$$b_{k} \equiv c_{-k\downarrow} c_{k\uparrow}

$$

We can rewrite the ground state as follows:

$$| \psi_{BCS \rangle} = \prod_{k} \left(1 + \phi_{k} b_{k}^\dagger\right) | 0 \rangle

$$

We can prove the commutation relation of b_{k} and b_{k}^\dagger as follows:

$$[b_{k}, b_{k'}^\dagger] = \delta_{k, k'}

$$

$$[(1-b_{k}), (1-b_{k'}^\dagger)] = \delta_{k, k'}

$$

So Cooper pair is a boson.

We go back to the normalization of the ground state:\begin{align}
    \braket{\psi_{BCS}}{\psi_{BCS}} & = \prod_{k} \langle 0 | 1 + \phi^*_{k \rangle b_{k}} \braket{1 + \phi_{k} b_{k}^\dagger}{0} \\
    & = \prod_{k} \langle 0 | 1 + \phi^*_{k b_{k}} + \phi_{k b_{k}^\dagger} + \phi_{k} \phi^*_{k} b_{k} b_{k}^\dagger | 0 \rangle \\
    & = \prod_{k} \langle 0 | 1 + \phi_{k \rangle \phi^*_{k} (1 - b_{k^\dagger b_{k}})} | 0 \rangle \\
    & = \prod_{k} (1 + \phi^2_{k})
\end{align}

Based on that we can get the expectation value of Cooper pair creation/annihilation operator:\begin{align}
    \langle \psi_{BCS |} b_{k}^\dagger | \psi_{BCS \rangle} & = \langle 0 | \prod_{k'} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) b_{k}^\dagger \prod_{k''} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) | 0 \rangle \\
    & = \langle 0 | \left(1 + \phi^*_{k} b_{k} \right) b^\dagger_{k} \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \prod_{k''} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) | 0 \rangle \\
    & = \langle 0 | \left( 1 + \phi^*_{k} b_{k} \right) b^\dagger_{k} \left( 1 + \phi_{k b^\dagger_{k}} \right) \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \left(1 + \phi_{k'} b_{k'}^\dagger\right)| 0 \rangle \\
    & = \langle 0 | \left(\phi^*_{k} \right) \left( 1 -  b^\dagger_{k  b_{k}} \right) \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \left(1 + \phi_{k'} b_{k'}^\dagger\right)| 0 \rangle \\
    & = \phi^*_{k} \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^2_{k'}\right)  \\
\end{align}

Similarly, we can get the expectation value of b_{k}:\begin{align}
    \langle \psi_{BCS |} b_{k} | \psi_{BCS \rangle} & = \langle 0 | \prod_{k'} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) b_{k} \prod_{k''} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) | 0 \rangle \\
    & = \langle 0 | \prod_{k'} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \prod_{k''\neq k} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) b_{k} \left(1 + \phi_{k} b_{k}^\dagger\right)| 0 \rangle \\
    & = \langle 0 | \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \prod_{k''\neq k} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) \left(1 + \phi^*_{k b_{k}}\right) \phi_{k} \left( 1 - b_{k^\dagger b_{k}}\right)| 0 \rangle \\
    & = \phi_{k} \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^2_{k'}\right)  \\
\end{align}

It might give us a hint that the average of field operator over a coherent state is the wave function.

And we can get a mean-field theory by averaging the 4-point interaction term over Cooper channel.\begin{align}
    \delta(b^\dagger_{k'})\delta(b_{k}) & = (b^\dagger_{k'} - \langle b^\dagger_{k' \rangle})(b_{k} - \langle b_{k \rangle}) \\
    & = b^\dagger_{k'} b_{k} - b^\dagger_{k'} \langle b_{k \rangle} - \langle b^\dagger_{k' \rangle} b_{k} + \langle b^\dagger_{k' \rangle} \langle b_{k \rangle} \\
    & \approx 0
\end{align}

So

$$b^\dagger_{k'} b_{k} \approx b^\dagger_{k'} \langle b_{k \rangle} + \langle b^\dagger_{k' \rangle} b_{k} - \langle b^\dagger_{k' \rangle} \langle b_{k \rangle}

$$

Giving  \langle b_{k \rangle}   (\langle b^\dagger_{k' \rangle} ) new names as gap function \Delta_{k} (\Delta^*_{k}), we can get the mean-field Hamiltonian:\begin{align}
    H  = & \sum_{\mathbf{k}, \sigma} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} + V^{\text{e-ph}}_{\mathbf{k}, \mathbf{k'}} c^\dagger_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow} c^\dagger_{\mathbf{k'}\uparrow} c_{-\mathbf{k'}\downarrow} c_{-\mathbf{k}\uparrow} \\
    \xrightarrow{\text{MF}} & \sum_{\mathbf{k}, \sigma} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} + \sum_{\mathbf{k}} \Delta_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-\mathbf{k}\downarrow}^\dagger + \Delta^*_{-\mathbf{k}} c_{-\mathbf{k}\downarrow} c_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow} + \Delta^*_{-\mathbf{k}} \Delta_{\mathbf{k}} \\
    \xrightarrow{\text{Nambu Spinor}} & \sum_{\mathbf{k}, \sigma} \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} (\vec{h}_{\mathbf{k}} \cdot \vec{\tau}) \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} + \Delta^*_{-\mathbf{k}} \Delta_{\mathbf{k}} \\
\end{align}

hierarchy.lvl2: Symmetry breaking of the MF Hamiltonian
Symmetry breaking of the MF Hamiltonian

records.content:
We will see here how the U(1) symmetry is broken by the mean-field Hamiltonian and how it leads to a breaking of conservation of particle number.

We will see that it preserve the SU(2) symmetry.

Spin-fluctuation can serve as a mediation of attractive potential between electrons, where phonon plays this role in conventional superconductors.

But here we want to see the interplay between spin-fluctuation and phonon mediation.

The best case is that spin-fluctuation is also kind of a quadratic form.

Or maybe it’s a quatic interactino but we take the mean-field approximation.
